questions.question: What does Geometric Algebra unify?
options: Vectors, complex numbers, quaternions, matrices, Plucker coordinates — all under one algebraic framework
questions.correctAnswer: Vectors, complex numbers, quaternions, matrices, Plucker coordinates — all under one algebraic framework
questions.explanation: GA: one algebra, one product (geometric product). Complex numbers = 2D even subalgebra. Quaternions = 3D even subalgebra. Unifies dot+cross.

questions.question: What is the geometric product of two vectors?
options: ab = a·b + a∧b — scalar (dot) PLUS bivector (wedge)
questions.correctAnswer: ab = a·b + a∧b — scalar (dot) PLUS bivector (wedge)
questions.explanation: Geometric product = symmetric part (dot, scalar) + antisymmetric part (wedge, bivector). Like complex numbers: real + imaginary.

questions.question: What are the grades in a 3D multivector?
options: Grade 0 (scalar), Grade 1 (vector, 3 components), Grade 2 (bivector, 3), Grade 3 (trivector, 1) — 2³=8 total
questions.correctAnswer: Grade 0 (scalar), Grade 1 (vector, 3 components), Grade 2 (bivector, 3), Grade 3 (trivector, 1) — 2³=8 total
questions.explanation: Pascal's triangle per dimension. 1,3,3,1 for 3D. Total = 2^dimension. Multivector = sum of all grades.

questions.question: Why is the cross product a 3D-only accident?
options: Cross product = Hodge dual of wedge: a×b = (a∧b)*. Only in 3D does the dual of a bivector give a vector. Wedge works in any dimension.
questions.correctAnswer: Cross product = Hodge dual of wedge: a×b = (a∧b)*. Only in 3D does the dual of a bivector give a vector. Wedge works in any dimension.
questions.explanation: In 3D: bivectors have 3 components = vectors have 3 components → duality possible. In 4D: bivectors have 6 components → can't map to 4-vector.

questions.e: 2D GA: basis {1, e₁, e₂, e₁₂}. Even subalgebra: {1, e₁₂}. e₁₂² = -1. This IS the complex numbers. i = e₁₂.
questions.explanation: In 3D, bivectors have 3 components (e₁₂, e₁₃, e₂₃) and vectors also have 3 components. Hodge dual maps between them: a×b = *(a∧b). In 4D, bivectors have 6 components — no dual to a 4-vector. The cross product only works in exactly 3 dimensions.
questions.question: How do complex numbers relate to GA?
options: Complex numbers ARE the even subalgebra of 2D GA — scalar + bivector. a+bi ↔ a + b·e₁₂.
questions.correctAnswer: Complex numbers ARE the even subalgebra of 2D GA — scalar + bivector. a+bi ↔ a + b·e₁₂.

questions.question: What is a rotor in Geometric Algebra?
options: The GA version of a quaternion — scalar + bivector. Rotates vectors via sandwich product: v' = RvR̃.
questions.correctAnswer: The GA version of a quaternion — scalar + bivector. Rotates vectors via sandwich product: v' = RvR̃.
questions.explanation: Rotor: R = cos(θ/2) - sin(θ/2)B where B is unit bivector. Same 4 components as quaternion. Isomorphic to quaternions.

questions.question: What does the sandwich product RvR̃ do?
options: Rotates vector v by twice the angle encoded in rotor R. R̃ is reverse (conjugate) of R.
questions.correctAnswer: Rotates vector v by twice the angle encoded in rotor R. R̃ is reverse (conjugate) of R.
questions.explanation: Sandwich = universal GA pattern. Reflection: -nvn. Rotation: RvR̃ (two reflections). Translation: TvT̃. Everything is a sandwich.

questions.question: How are rotors more general than quaternions?
options: Rotors work in ANY dimension — 2D (complex numbers), 3D (quaternions), 4D+. Quaternions only in 3D.
questions.correctAnswer: Rotors work in ANY dimension — 2D (complex numbers), 3D (quaternions), 4D+. Quaternions only in 3D.
questions.explanation: Dimension-independence is the rotor's key advantage. 2D rotor = 2 components (complex). 3D rotor = 4 components (quaternion). 4D rotor = 8 components.

questions.e: Wedge product = 0 iff vectors are linearly dependent (parallel). a∧b = |a||b|sin(θ)·B. sin(0)=0, sin(180)=0.
questions.explanation: a∧b = 0 when vectors are linearly dependent — they point in the same or opposite direction. The parallelogram they span has zero area. sin(0)=sin(π)=0, so |a||b|sin(θ)=0.
questions.question: What does a∧b = 0 mean geometrically?
options: a and b are collinear (parallel) — they span zero area
questions.correctAnswer: a and b are collinear (parallel) — they span zero area

questions.e: I = Hodge dual operator. v·I converts vector to bivector. I²=-1 makes it behave like imaginary unit. Basis of complex/quaternion behavior in GA.
questions.explanation: I = e₁₂₃ is the unit volume element. I² = -1 makes it behave like the imaginary unit i. Multiplying a vector by I: v·I converts to bivector; multiplying a bivector by I: B·I converts to vector. This is the Hodge dual operation.
questions.question: What is the pseudoscalar I in 3D GA?
options: I = e₁₂₃ — the volume element. I² = -1 (in Euclidean). Multiplying by I flips grade (vector↔bivector, scalar↔trivector).
questions.correctAnswer: I = e₁₂₃ — the volume element. I² = -1 (in Euclidean). Multiplying by I flips grade (vector↔bivector, scalar↔trivector).

questions.question: What is Projective Geometric Algebra (PGA)?
options: Models 3D Euclidean geometry in a 4D algebra with degenerate metric (e₀²=0). Points=trivectors, lines=bivectors, planes=vectors.
questions.correctAnswer: Models 3D Euclidean geometry in a 4D algebra with degenerate metric (e₀²=0). Points=trivectors, lines=bivectors, planes=vectors.
questions.explanation: PGA: adds projective dimension. Points are intersections of 3 planes. Lines = join of 2 points. Ray-plane intersection = meet(L,Π). One operation.

questions.question: What is a motor in PGA?
options: Rigid body motion (rotation + translation) as one object. Motor = even-grade element. Acts via sandwich. Generalizes dual quaternions.
questions.correctAnswer: Rigid body motion (rotation + translation) as one object. Motor = even-grade element. Acts via sandwich. Generalizes dual quaternions.
questions.explanation: Motor slerp: one interpolation for rotation AND translation. No separate lerp(pos) + slerp(quat). Unified rigid body interpolation.

questions.question: How does PGA express ray-plane intersection?
options: L ∨ Π — the meet (regressive product) of a line L and plane Π is their intersection point. One algebraic product.
questions.correctAnswer: L ∨ Π — the meet (regressive product) of a line L and plane Π is their intersection point. One algebraic product.
questions.explanation: Join (∧) creates higher grade. Meet (∨) = intersection. Ray = join(camera, farPoint). Hit = meet(ray, plane). All algebra, no special cases.

questions.e: PGA = Cl(3,0,1) — degenerate metric. e₀²=0 enables translation via rotor-like sandwich. Same algebra models points, lines, planes, and rigid motions.
questions.explanation: e₀ is a null basis vector (e₀²=0). This degenerate metric is the mathematical trick that encodes translation in PGA. Null vectors represent ideal points at infinity; their exponential generates translations just as bivector exponentials generate rotations.
questions.question: Why is e₀² = 0 in PGA?
options: e₀ is a null vector representing the ideal point at origin — encodes translation. Null vectors are the key to modeling Euclidean geometry projectively.
questions.correctAnswer: e₀ is a null vector representing the ideal point at origin — encodes translation. Null vectors are the key to modeling Euclidean geometry projectively.

questions.e: Motor = even-grade element in PGA. 8 components: 1 scalar, 3 e₀ᵢ bivectors (translation), 3 eᵢⱼ bivectors (rotation), 1 e₀₁₂₃. Represents rigid body motion.
questions.explanation: A PGA motor has 8 components: 1 scalar + 3 e₀ᵢ bivectors (translation generators) + 3 eᵢⱼ bivectors (rotation generators) + 1 e₀₁₂₃ pseudoscalar. It is isomorphic to a dual quaternion and represents any rigid body motion.
questions.question: How many components does a PGA motor have?
options: 8 (scalar + 6 bivectors + pseudoscalar) — isomorphic to dual quaternion
questions.correctAnswer: 8 (scalar + 6 bivectors + pseudoscalar) — isomorphic to dual quaternion

questions.question: What makes A* different from Dijkstra's algorithm?
options: A* uses a heuristic h(n) estimating distance to goal — guides search toward target. Dijkstra is h(n)=0 (explores all directions).
questions.correctAnswer: A* uses a heuristic h(n) estimating distance to goal — guides search toward target. Dijkstra is h(n)=0 (explores all directions).
questions.explanation: f(n) = g(n) + h(n). g = cost from start. h = estimated cost to goal. If h is admissible (never overestimates), A* finds optimal path.

questions.question: What makes a heuristic admissible for A*?
options: h(n) must never overestimate the true remaining cost — typically Euclidean distance for spatial grids
questions.correctAnswer: h(n) must never overestimate the true remaining cost — typically Euclidean distance for spatial grids
questions.explanation: Admissible = optimistic. Euclidean distance ≤ actual path distance. Manhattan distance on grid = admissible (but not on diagonals).

questions.question: What data structure makes A* efficient?
options: Priority queue (min-heap) — always expand the node with lowest f(n) first
questions.correctAnswer: Priority queue (min-heap) — always expand the node with lowest f(n) first
questions.explanation: Open set = min-heap by f(n). Extract-min gives best candidate. Closed set = visited nodes. O(b^d) worst case; heuristic reduces this dramatically.

questions.e: Navmesh: convex polygons = nodes. Shared edges = connections. Much fewer nodes than grid cells. UE generates navmesh from level geometry.
questions.explanation: Navmesh drastically reduces search space compared to grids — dozens of convex polygons vs thousands of grid cells. A* on navmesh searches polygon connectivity graph. UE generates navmeshes using Recast.
questions.question: What is a navigation mesh (navmesh)?
options: A polygon mesh representing walkable areas — A* finds paths along mesh edges/vertices, much fewer nodes than grids
questions.correctAnswer: A polygon mesh representing walkable areas — A* finds paths along mesh edges/vertices, much fewer nodes than grids

questions.e: Navmesh drastically reduces search space. UE's Recast/Detour generates navmeshes. Pathfinding on navmesh = string-pulling for smooth final path.
questions.explanation: Dijkstra = A* with h(n)=0. Without heuristic guidance, Dijkstra explores equally in all directions. A* uses Euclidean/Manhattan distance to guide search toward the goal — dramatically fewer expanded nodes.
questions.question: What is the difference between A* on a grid vs a navmesh?
options: Grid: thousands of cells, Manhattan/Euclidean heuristic. Navmesh: dozens of polygons, Euclidean heuristic between centroids + edge costs. Navmesh is much faster.
questions.correctAnswer: Grid: thousands of cells, Manhattan/Euclidean heuristic. Navmesh: dozens of polygons, Euclidean heuristic between centroids + edge costs. Navmesh is much faster.
